issue_series.name: Denmark classic bicolors
issue_series.description: Perforated bicolor definitives and numeral designs, late 19th century.

scott_catalog_entries.description: Illustrative Denmark bicolor definitive tied to sample inventory; see stamp_catalog_number for Michel cross-reference.
scott_catalog_entries.notes: Illustrative primary listing for the sample stamp; see stamp_catalog_number for Michel cross-ref.

numeral_cancels.history: Original assignment to Flensborg. Following the defeat in the Second Schleswig War (1864), the town was ceded to Prussia/Austria, and the Danish numeral cancel was retired from this location.
Reassigned to the Sjælland town of Skibby during the post-war reorganization of the postal system to fill the gaps left by lost Schleswig territories.

issue_series.name: 1847 First General Issue
issue_series.description: First U.S. nationwide adhesive postage issue (Franklin and Washington).

issue_series.name: 1875 Reprints of 1847 Issue
issue_series.description: BEP reprints of the original 1847 designs, produced mainly for collectors and archives.

issue_series.name: 1851-1856 Imperforate Definitives
issue_series.description: Imperforate 1851-era definitive issue and related early denominations/types.

issue_series.name: 1857-1860 Perforated Definitives
issue_series.description: Perforated continuation of classic 1851 designs with type and plate distinctions.

issue_series.name: 1857 Reprints
issue_series.description: Reprint-related classic listings in the 40-47 block.

issue_series.name: 1861-1866 Civil War Definitives
issue_series.description: National Bank Note Company issue replacing prior designs during and after Civil War contract changes.

issue_series.name: 1867-1868 Grilled Issue
issue_series.description: D/E/F and Z grill-era classic definitives and related varieties.

scott_catalog_entries.description: First United States adhesive postage stamp (1847 general issue): 5¢ Benjamin Franklin line engraving on bluish wove paper; imperforate, for the domestic single-rate up to 300 miles.
1847 first general issue: 10¢ George Washington line engraving; imperforate, for higher domestic letter rates and some foreign mail.
1875 Bureau of Engraving and Printing hard-paper reprint of the 1847 5¢ Franklin (original Scott 1); imperforate special printing for collectors and archives.
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the 1847 10¢ Washington (original Scott 2); imperforate line-engraved reissue.
1851 1¢ Benjamin Franklin Type I blue shades; imperforate line-engraved stamp from the first U.S. denominationally complete issue.
1851 1¢ Franklin Type Ia; imperforate stamp with fuller outer framelines than the later Type II printings.
1851 1¢ Franklin Type II blue shades; imperforate workhorse 1¢ from multiple early plates with reduced or broken outer frame lines.
1851 1¢ Franklin Type III; imperforate stamp distinguished by breaks in the top and bottom outer frame lines.
1851 1¢ Franklin Type IV; imperforate late-plate state with heavy recutting along the frame lines.
1851 3¢ George Washington Type I orange brown; imperforate standard domestic letter-rate denomination of the 1851 series.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: dull red, pinkish, claret, brownish carmine and shades in between
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1851 3¢ Washington Type I in dull red, claret, and related shades; imperforate line-engraved domestic letter stamp.
1855–56 5¢ Thomas Jefferson Type I red brown shades; imperforate stamp from the 1855–61 middle-value issue.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: green, dark green or yellowish green
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1855 10¢ George Washington Type I green; imperforate line-engraved stamp for the long-haul domestic ten-cent letter rate.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: green, dark green or yellowish green
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1855 10¢ Washington Type II green; imperforate issue differing from Type I in recutting and frame-line details around the portrait.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: green, dark green or yellowish green
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1855 10¢ Washington Type III green; imperforate intermediate plate state of the 1855 ten-cent engraving.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: green, dark green or yellowish green
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1855 10¢ Washington Type IV green; imperforate late state of the 1855 ten-cent design with extensive plate wear and recutting.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: black, intense black or gray black
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1851 12¢ George Washington black; imperforate high-value line engraving for registered or heavy domestic postage.
1861 1¢ Benjamin Franklin Type I blue; first National Bank Note Company perforated issue (15–15½) of the Civil War-era letter-rate design.
1857–61 1¢ Franklin Type Ia blue; perforated 15–15½ stamp retaining complete outer framelines like Type I.
1857–61 1¢ Franklin Type II blue; perforated 15–15½ issue with cropped or incomplete outer frame lines compared with Type I.
1857–61 1¢ Franklin Type III blue; perforated 15–15½ stamp with characteristic breaks in the outer top and bottom frame lines.
1857–61 1¢ Franklin Type IIIa blue; perforated 15–15½ intermediate plate state between Types III and IV.
1857–61 1¢ Franklin Type IV blue; perforated 15–15½ issue with pronounced recutting along the top and bottom frame lines.
1857–61 1¢ Franklin Type V blue; most common perforated 1¢ Franklin, with side ornaments trimmed for tight perforation spacing.
1857 3¢ George Washington Type I rose shades; perforated 15 or 15½ domestic letter stamp from the first U.S. perforated issue.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: dull red, brownish-claret, plum, and brownish-carmine
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1857 3¢ Washington Type II dull red through plum shades; perforated 15 or 15½ stamp with plate work that removes portions of the outer frame lines.
1858 5¢ Thomas Jefferson Type I brick red; perforated 15 or 15½ line-engraved Jefferson from the 1857 perforated series.
1857 5¢ Jefferson Type I red brown and pale red brown shades; perforated 15 or 15½ early Jefferson of the perforated issue.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: brown, pale brown or deep brown
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1859 5¢ Jefferson Type I brown shades; perforated 15 or 15½ late Type I plate printing before adoption of the Type II frame layout.
1861 5¢ Jefferson Type II orange brown; perforated 15 or 15½ design without the small projections at top and bottom of the earlier Type I.
1861 5¢ Jefferson Type II brown (listed variety "A"); perforated 15 or 15½ companion shade to the orange brown Type II printing.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: dark green, green, bluish green, yellowish green
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1857–61 10¢ George Washington Type I green; perforated 15½ line engraving for the transcontinental ten-cent letter rate.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: dark green, green, bluish green, yellowish green
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1857–61 10¢ Washington Type II green; perforated 15½ issue with plate alterations distinct from the Type I frame line pattern.

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: dark green, green, bluish green, yellowish green

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: dark green, green, bluish green, yellowish green

scott_catalog_entries.primary_color: dark green, green, bluish green, yellowish green
scott_catalog_entries.description: 1857–61 10¢ Washington Type V green; perforated 15½ final major plate state of the perforated ten-cent issue.
1857 12¢ George Washington black; perforated 15½ high-value line engraving for registry and heavy postage.
1860 24¢ Washington gray lilac; perforated 15½ stamp for large domestic frankings and some international rates.
1860 30¢ Benjamin Franklin orange shades; perforated 15½ high-denomination line engraving.
1860 90¢ George Washington deep blue; perforated 15½ rare high value for very heavy letter postage and certain steamship uses.
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the perforated 1¢ Franklin, perf 12 (original 1861–67 printings were 15–15½).
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the 3¢ Washington, perf 12 (original perforated issue was 15–15½).
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the 5¢ Jefferson, perf 12 (original perforated issue was 15–15½).
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the 10¢ Washington, perf 12 (original perforated issue was 15½).
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the 12¢ Washington, perf 12 (original perforated issue was 15½).
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the 24¢ Washington, perf 12 (original perforated issue was 15½).
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the 30¢ Franklin, perf 12 (original perforated issue was 15½).
1875 BEP hard-paper reprint of the 90¢ Washington, perf 12 (original perforated issue was 15½).

5c Thomas Jefferson. 1857-61 Issue. Printed by Toppan, Carpenter & Co. This variety (Type II) features the 'Brick Red' color and is distinguished by the projections at the top and bottom being partially cut away to accommodate the perforation 15.

5c Thomas Jefferson. 1857-61 Issue. Type II. Distinguishable from #48 by its lighter, warmer brown shade. The design features the same 'Type II' characteristics where top and bottom ornaments are partially cut away to accommodate the perfs.

5c Thomas Jefferson. 1857-61 Issue. Type II. This is the rarest color variety of the perforated 5c Jefferson. The deep, dark brown shade is distinct from the 'Bright Brown' of #49. Design features partially cut away ornaments at top and bottom.

10c George Washington. 1857-61 Issue. Type II. The design is complete at the top (top line and lines above X are full), but the bottom shell ornaments are partially cut away and the bottom line is broken in the center.

10c George Washington. 1857-61 Issue. Type III. Both the top and bottom lines are broken. The shells at the top and bottom are also partially missing. A key identification point for your AI's edge-detection logic.

10c George Washington. 1857-61 Issue. Type IV. This variety occurs when the top or bottom lines (or both) have been recut. Your OpenCV model should look for a 'stronger' or thicker line compared to the original engraving.

10c George Washington. 1857-61 Issue. Type V. The most common type. The side ornaments are partially trimmed to allow for more space between stamps during the perforation process. Side pearls are typically missing or incomplete.
1861 1¢ Benjamin Franklin indigo; National Bank Note Company issue on tinted paper, perf 12, opening value of the 1861 general issue.
1861 3¢ George Washington brown rose to deep red; National Bank Note Company perf 12 Civil War letter-rate stamp.
1861 5¢ Thomas Jefferson brown; National Bank Note Company perf 12 middle denomination.

lookup_numeral_cancel.description: Look up a postal numeral cancel assignment by country and numeral. Returns matching town/date-window rows from the local xFrame consolidated data; optional Scott number helps infer the stamp issue date.

parameters.description: Cancel digits only (integer < 999): the numeral in the cancel, not year/codes/letters.
Scott # if known for this stamp; omit otherwise.

chatgpt_suggested_stamp_listing.brief: eBay-style stamp listing with strong collector appeal, Scott anchoring, and authoritative cancel lookup
template_lines: You are helping a professional stamp dealer create an eBay listing.
GOAL:
- Produce a listing that is accurate, confident, and appealing to collectors.
- Combine careful identification with natural dealer-style writing.
CORE RULE:
- Be conservative in FACTS, but confident in PRESENTATION.
FACT SAFETY RULES (STRICT):
- Do NOT guess or invent catalog numbers, watermark varieties, printings, shades, or cancel locations.
- Only state such details if clearly supported by the image or authoritative inputs.
- If uncertain, briefly qualify the specific detail (e.g., 'appears to be', 'not verified').
- Do NOT over-explain uncertainty or repeat disclaimers.
- Treat filename and notes as hints unless covered by rules below.
SCOTT NUMBER HANDLING (IMPORTANT):
- If a Scott catalog number (e.g., 'Scott #31', 'sc0031') appears in the filename, treat it as correct for the base issue.
- Use it confidently to populate country, denomination, and general issue description.
- You may include standard, well-known characteristics of that issue (design, typical colors, era).
- Do NOT assume or invent sub-varieties (printing number, watermark orientation, rare types) unless independently supported.
CANCEL LOOKUP HANDLING (AUTHORITATIVE):
- A cancel lookup may be provided as structured data.
- If present, treat it as authoritative.
- Use the provided town name in the listing.
- Reflect the provided confidence level in natural language:
* high → state normally
* medium → use 'likely'
* low → use 'possibly' or 'not confirmed'
- Do NOT override or contradict the lookup.
HIGH-RISK AREAS (handle carefully):
- Perforations: describe condition naturally; only give counts if clearly measurable.
- Watermarks / printings: only include if clearly proven; otherwise omit or lightly qualify.
IMPORTANT:
- Separate FACTS from PRESENTATION.
- Facts must be accurate and controlled.
- Presentation (title and description) should be confident, natural, and appealing.
- Do NOT let uncertainty make the writing dull — only qualify the specific detail.
INPUTS:
Commit summary:
__FIRST_LINE__
Metadata JSON:
__META_JSON__
Cancel lookup (authoritative):
__CANCEL_LOOKUP_JSON__
Target price context:
__TARGET_JSON__
Dealer notes:
__NOTES_JSON__
STEP 1 — KEY FACTS (concise, grounded):
- Country, denomination, and issue (use Scott number if present)
- Catalog number (state confidently if from filename)
- Brief standard description of the issue (design, color, era)
- Cancel: describe marking; include lookup town if provided
- Condition: centering, perforations, visible faults
STEP 2 — EBAY TITLE:
- Natural, collector-friendly, and specific
- Include Scott number, country, denomination, and notable visible features
- Include cancel town if lookup confidence is high or medium
- Avoid speculative or unverified claims
STEP 3 — DESCRIPTION (main selling section):
- Write like an experienced stamp dealer
- Highlight eye appeal: cancel clarity, color contrast, centering, overall presentation
- Use confident language for known facts
- Integrate dealer notes ONLY if consistent with evidence
- Handle uncertainty lightly and locally
- When a town is included, avoid speculative historical claims unless confidence is high
- Do NOT mention metadata, AI, or image limitations
STEP 4 — CONDITION SUMMARY:
- One clean, dealer-style line summarizing condition
STYLE GUIDELINES:
- Avoid robotic or report-like tone
- Avoid excessive hedging
- Sound knowledgeable and trustworthy
- Prioritize readability and buyer confidence
OUTPUT FORMAT:
[Title]
[Key Facts]
[Condition]
[Description]

You write stamp descriptions in the voice and density of Jay Smith & Associates–style specialized listings: catalog-precise, narrative-rich, and confident. Technical identifiers and bracketed issue specs come first; condition is honest; rarity and literature support the ask. Do not invent catalog numbers, print quantities, sheet positions, expert quotes, or handbook facts—use only what the image, dealer notes, and uploader JSON plausibly support; where unknown, say so briefly or omit.
Commit summary line (from uploader):
__FIRST_LINE__
Full structured parameters from the upload form (JSON — use every field that helps identification or copy):
__META_JSON__
Dealer target listing price (US$ context for retail; do not invent a catalog value): __TARGET_JSON__
Dealer notes and hints (weave into identification and narrative): __NOTES_JSON__
TARGET STYLE (match this shape and rhythm; adapt facts to the actual stamp):

Open with the primary catalog and variety headline, e.g. leading with Scott when that is your default, then the key variety in readable caps (e.g. INVERTED FRAME) when the notes/image justify it. Immediately follow with a single square-bracket block that packs the issue line: year or era, printing or plate generation in parentheses when relevant, denomination and color description (you may capitalize a decisive color word such as ULTRAMARINE when it signals a printing), explicit perforation (e.g. Perforation 14 x 13.5), and any repeated specialty phrase that anchors the variety.

Then continue in the same flow: mint/used, centering grade (F-VF, VF+, etc.), and cancellation type (e.g. proper type of 4-ring mute cancellation) when visible. If there are faults, use a pivot like “but with defects” and then a frank paragraph: what looks fine at a glance versus what shows under scrutiny (rounded perf tip, thin, creases, marginal tears). Balance honesty with proportion—e.g. defects obvious only under close inspection when that matches the image.

When rarity is justified by the notes, expand with a specialist essay: quoted emphasis sparingly (e.g. “VERY RARE”, “rarely”) only if defensible; sheet/print arithmetic, positions, survival, and time-to-discovery narratives when the dealer material supplies them. Weave dated literature in-line: Author [year] with short paraphrase or attributed quote; name surveys (archives, plate reconstructions) and decades of study when notes support it—never fabricate citations.

Add comparative scarcity (Used vs Mint, variety vs normal frame, etc.) when notes allow. For catalog values, mirror the example’s clarity: name each catalog with edition year, value in its native unit, optional parenthetical US$ equivalent with month/year of the conversion when helpful, then other catalogs (Facit, AFA, Scott, etc.) in separate sentences. If the dealer gave a target price, you may relate it to catalog context without claiming it is a catalog value.

Close with a strong rarity line when earned, optional certificate or guarantee language only if the notes mention it, and optional dealer stock history (“only example since …”) only if the uploader/dealer text supports it.
STRUCTURE RULES:
- Default catalog lead: Scott unless the notes specify otherwise.
- One bracketed `[…]` issue specification early; keep it dense but readable.
- Prefer one sustained BODY after the opening line (or after TITLE + blank line per OUTPUT FORMAT) rather than bullet lists.
- ALL CAPS: use only for genuine emphasis points (variety name, critical color, RARE), not every other word.
OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) TITLE — one line: catalog-forward opening matching the target style above (may be long; may include the bracket block on this line).
2) Blank line.

3) BODY — continuation in the same voice: defects, rarity and history, valuations, closing flourish, certificate/stock notes if supported; end with a single line “Asking context:” and the dealer’s target price if provided, or say price not specified.

multi_stamp_sheet_xframe.brief: Many stamps in one image → xFrame catalog JSON
template_lines: You are a philatelic cataloging assistant. The attached image may show MULTIPLE postage stamps on one scan or photo (e.g. a stock sheet, album page, or black-background layout). Your job is to detect every distinct stamp impression, read what is visible on each, and fill the response JSON.

The API returns one object with a "stamp" array only: one stamp object per detected impression, matching the response schema. Use the stamp_id rules below (country + Scott + local index); they must stay unique within this response.
Commit summary line (from uploader):
__FIRST_LINE__
Full structured parameters from the upload form (JSON):
__META_JSON__
Dealer target listing price (JSON; may be null — often one price for the whole sheet): __TARGET_JSON__
Dealer notes and hints (JSON; may be null): __NOTES_JSON__
=== VISUAL WORKFLOW ===
1) Segment mentally: treat each physically separate perforated (or cut) stamp as one item. Ignore the background between stamps.
2) Reading order: top-to-bottom, then left-to-right (row-major), so stamp numbering matches a human scanning the grid.
3) For each stamp, extract what the image supports:
- Country or postal administration (e.g. from script, language, or emblem text such as SVERIGE).
- Face value: numerals in side tablets or corners, and currency unit from text (öre, kr, cent, etc.).
- Design / series clues: coat of arms, portrait, commemorative topic, "Official" / service types, color shade when it distinguishes catalog varieties.
- Cancellations: postmark town or route, readable date (day/month/year when possible). If a day is clear, you may note the weekday in stamp.summary.
- Condition and centering: infer from the crop (faults, thins, heavy cancel obscuring design, margin balance). Use schema enums only.
- Perforation: describe visible perf or roulettes, damage, or "imperf" / "unknown" in summary when not clear.
4) When several stamps clearly belong to the same issue or definitive series (same design type and era), say so in each stamp's summary.
5) If two stamps look like the same denomination but different printings or colors, keep them as separate stamp rows and explain the distinction in summary.
=== STAMP IDS (REQUIRED) ===

Every stamp row MUST have a unique stamp_id. Build each stamp_id from three segments separated by a single hyphen "-" (use lowercase ASCII letters, digits, underscores inside scott_key only, and digits for the index):
1) country_id — the same issuing-country slug you set on that stamp row (ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 when clear, otherwise a short mnemonic like "se", "ddr"). If the country cannot be determined, use "xx".

2) scott_key — a single slug derived from the primary Scott listing you identify for that impression (match how you fill stamp.scott): main numeral as digits; append an optional variety letter (a–f) with no separator; if the listing uses a Scott section prefix (e.g. B, RW), use that section in lowercase, one underscore, then the numeral (examples: "500", "500c", "rw_4", "b_12a"). If you cannot assign a Scott number, use "unknown".

3) local_index — a positive integer starting at 1, counting only among stamps on this image that share the same country_id and the same scott_key, in the same top-to-bottom, left-to-right reading order as the stamp rows. The first stamp with (us, 500) gets local_index 1; a second US 500 on the same sheet gets 2; a US 501 on the same sheet gets 1 again.

Concatenate: {country_id}-{scott_key}-{local_index}. Examples: "us-500-1", "us-500-2", "us-rw_4-1", "se-unknown-1" (unknown Scott: still use a per-(country, unknown) counter in reading order so duplicates of "unknown" on one country stay unique).
Do not include the upload basename, timestamp, or image hash inside stamp_id.
=== PRICING ===

Do not split a single dealer target price across stamps unless the notes explicitly say so. If one price applies to the whole sheet, mention it in the first stamp's summary (or omit if the schema has no listing fields).

numeral_cancel_identification.brief: Numeral cancel identification with cautious eBay-style listing (plain text)
template_lines: You are helping a stamp dealer prepare an accurate eBay listing for this upload.
Your first priority is factual accuracy and uncertainty handling.
Do not invent catalog numbers, cancels, perforation gauges, towns, dates, or varieties.
If evidence is incomplete, explicitly say so.
Do not infer a full cancel number from a partial digit unless supported by lookup results and image evidence.
Commit summary line (from uploader):
__FIRST_LINE__
Full structured parameters from the upload form (JSON — use every field that helps identification or listing copy):
__META_JSON__
Dealer target listing price (use as pricing context; do not invent a Scott value): __TARGET_JSON__
Dealer notes and hints (incorporate into identification and description): __NOTES_JSON__
When evaluating numeral cancels:
- Use lookup tools when available instead of relying only on visual interpretation
- Aim for one tool round: decide the country, numeral reading, and any optional filters before calling; avoid serial guess-and-retry loops

- Each tool reply is a JSON envelope: on success use the object in "result" (e.g. rows under keys like numeral_cancel); on failure read "error" (e.g. missing mandatory parameters). Fields "success", "retryable", and "final" summarize outcome — treat "final": true as the definitive answer for that call
- Treat lookup failures as negative evidence against the reading
- If "success" is false or "result" has zero matches for the reading you tested, do not present that numeral attribution as fact
- If the image suggests additional digits beyond the initial OCR result, reconsider the reading before calling, not after several partial lookups
- Prefer full centered numeral interpretations over isolated visible digits when supported by the image
- When multiple numeral assignments exist across time periods, compare them against the issue date before selecting one
When evaluating perforations:
- Do not claim an exact perforation gauge unless the image resolution and scale support it
- Avoid overconfident differentiation between close catalog varieties from image inspection alone
- If uncertain, state that perforation measurement is required
When evaluating dates:
- If a cancel date is readable, verify it is chronologically possible for the issue
- If the date precedes the issue date, treat the stamp identification or cancel reading as suspect
- If the date is sufficiently clear, include the day of the week
When evaluating towns or cities:
- Only identify a city when supported by the cancel text or lookup data
- Do not fabricate postal history context
- If a city is confirmed, you may briefly describe its historical regional role
From the stamp image, identify the issue where possible. Provide:
- A concise eBay title
- Scott or main catalog number when justified, otherwise explain uncertainty
- A condition report visible from the image
- Details on perforations, including visible faults or missing perforation teeth if observable
- Details on the cancel, including uncertainty level and lookup support
- A chronology check between issue date and cancel date if applicable
- Listing body text that respects the dealer's notes and target price band
Prefer cautious accuracy over confident speculation.
You are helping a stamp dealer prepare an eBay listing for this upload.
Commit summary line (from uploader):
__FIRST_LINE__
Full structured parameters from the upload form (JSON — use every field that helps identification or listing copy):
__META_JSON__
Dealer target listing price (use as pricing context; do not invent a Scott value): __TARGET_JSON__
Dealer notes and hints (incorporate into identification and description): __NOTES_JSON__
From the stamp image, identify the issue where possible. Provide:
- A concise eBay title
- Scott or main catalog number when you can justify it, or say when uncertain
- A condition report visible from the image
- Details on perforations, if any, such as missing or damaged ones, and the count of horizontal and vertical dents
- Details on the cancel, if any, such as city name or date of cancellation. If the date is clear enough, check that it is after the date of issue and cite the day of the week it represents
- If the city is clear enough, lookup its population at the time of the cancel or issue and describe its role in the region
- Listing body text that respects the dealer's notes and is consistent with their target price band (polite, professional tone)

stamp_numeral_cancel_identification.brief: Focused numeral-cancel identification from stamp image
template_lines: You are identifying the numeral cancel on a single stamp image.
Primary task: determine the FULL numeral cancel, not merely the clearest digit.
Try very hard to recover all visible digits.
Do not stop at a single digit if the strike may contain adjacent digits.
Image file name, uploader notes, and commit text are weak hints only. Do not let them determine the numeral.
Structured upload metadata:
__META_JSON__
Dealer notes:
__NOTES_JSON__
Instructions:
- Inspect the entire cancel impression, especially faint digits to the left and right of the darkest digit.
- Consider whether the apparent digit is only the center, left, or right part of a longer numeral.
- Look for partial loops, stems, serifs, tops, bottoms, and spacing consistent with adjacent digits.
- Prefer full multi-digit readings when the geometry supports them.
- Produce multiple candidates when uncertain.
- Assign confidence to each candidate.
- Do not identify a town from the numeral unless a lookup tool supports it.
- If a lookup for a candidate returns zero matches, treat that as negative evidence and reconsider partial or longer readings.
- If the stamp issue date is known, use it only after numeral candidates are generated, to rank period-compatible matches.
Required output JSON:
"visible_cancel_type": "numeral | barred_numeral | circular_date_stamp | unclear | none",
"full_numeral_best_reading": "string or null",
"digit_analysis": {
"left_side": "what digits or partial marks may be present",
"center": "what the clearest digit(s) appear to be",
"right_side": "what digits or partial marks may be present",
"spacing_geometry": "why the reading is one digit or multiple digits"
"candidates": [
"numeral": "string",
"confidence": 0.0,
"visual_basis": "specific visible evidence",
"uncertainties": "specific doubts"
"lookup_requests": [
"country": "dk",
"number": "candidate numeral as integer when applicable"
"do_not_claim": [
"town name unless lookup confirms it",
"single-digit numeral if adjacent marks plausibly form a longer number",
"catalog variety from cancel alone"
"short_human_summary": "plain-English summary of the best numeral reading and uncertainty"
Important failure rule:
If the clearest visible digit is only part of a larger centered strike, the answer must identify the larger candidate, not just the clearest digit.
You are a philatelic cataloging assistant. From the stamp image and the dealer context below, produce structured data for THIS stamp only.
The API returns JSON shaped as one object with a "stamp" array; each element is a stamp row per the response schema. For a single stamp use one element with a unique stamp_id.
Commit summary line (from uploader):
__FIRST_LINE__
Full structured parameters from the upload form (JSON):
__META_JSON__
Dealer target listing price (JSON; may be null): __TARGET_JSON__
Dealer notes and hints (JSON; may be null): __NOTES_JSON__

google_gemini.notes: GEMINI_API_KEY. Client: @google/genai; api_base_url documents the REST v1beta base the SDK targets.

openrouter.notes: OPENROUTER_API_KEY. Optional: OPENROUTER_HTTP_REFERER, OPENROUTER_TITLE for OpenRouter rankings headers.

B.description: Stamps sold for a premium (e.g., 10c+2c) for charity.

C.description: (Also called Air Post) Specifically for airmail service.

D.description: For mail sent via pressurized underground tubes (common in Italy).

E.description: For immediate delivery upon arrival at the post office.

G.description: Used to pay fees for insuring contents.

H.description: Paid for a return receipt to the sender.

I.description: Paid for mail posted after the daily collection deadline.

J.description: Collected from the recipient for unpaid postage.

K.description: Issued for use in a country's post offices in foreign cities.

L.description: Valid only for delivery within a specific city or district.

M.description: Issued for use by armed forces.

N.description: Issued by a conquering power in an occupied territory.

O.description: Restricted for use by government agencies only.

Q.description: Specifically for the transport of packages.

QE.description: Fast-tracked handling for parcel post (but not "Special Delivery").

R.description: Technically for taxes/fees, but included in Scott catalogs.

RW.description: The "Duck Stamps" (often found in the US Specialized catalog).
